name: Jack-o'-lantern
description: The Jack-o'-lantern is a cultural icon deeply rooted in Irish folklore, later popularized in North America. Originating from the tale of Stingy Jack, a man who tricked the Devil multiple times, the Jack-o'-lantern represents a soul denied entry into both Heaven and Hell. Condemned to wander the earth for eternity, Jack was given a burning coal by the Devil, which he placed inside a carved-out turnip to light his way. This turnip lantern became a symbol of lost souls and was initially used to ward off evil spirits, particularly on Samhain night. When Irish immigrants arrived in America, they found pumpkins to be a more suitable alternative to turnips due to their size and abundance. The practice of carving faces into pumpkins and placing candles inside became a widespread Halloween tradition, evolving from a cautionary tale into a festive symbol of the holiday.
subtitle: A wandering soul's lantern.
appearance: A carved pumpkin, typically with a grotesque or smiling face, illuminated from within by a candle or other light source. The size and shape of the pumpkin can vary, but the defining feature is the light emanating from the carved openings, creating a spooky and often unsettling visage.
habitat: Traditionally, Jack-o'-lanterns are found near homes, particularly during Halloween. In folklore, they were said to wander lonely roads and bogs. Modernly, they are ubiquitous in gardens, porches, and windows during the autumn season.
behavior: The Jack-o'-lantern is generally a passive object, though in folklore, it is associated with mischievous spirits and the potential to lead travelers astray. It serves as a visual representation of the boundary between the living and the dead, and its presence is meant to both ward off evil and acknowledge the spirits that may be present during Samhain/Halloween.

keyMyths.title: The Legend of Stingy Jack
keyMyths.description: The most famous tale tells of Stingy Jack, a notorious trickster who repeatedly outsmarted the Devil. Upon his death, Jack was denied entry into both Heaven and Hell due to his sinful life and his trickery of the Devil. As punishment, he was condemned to wander the earth with only a burning coal inside a carved-out turnip to light his way. This is the origin of the Jack-o'-lantern.

keyMyths.title: Will-o'-the-Wisps and Jack-o'-lanterns
keyMyths.description: Jack-o'-lanterns are often associated with Will-o'-the-wisps, mysterious lights seen over marshes and bogs. Some believe that Jack-o'-lanterns are a representation of these mischievous spirits, luring travelers off the path and into danger. The flickering light and unsettling appearance contribute to this association.

keyMyths.title: Samhain and the Spirits
keyMyths.description: During Samhain (now Halloween), the veil between the living and the dead is believed to be thin. Jack-o'-lanterns were originally used as a way to ward off evil spirits and other supernatural entities that might roam the earth on this night. The carved faces and flickering light were meant to frighten away unwanted visitors.
keyMyths.source: Halloween: From Pagan Ritual to Party Night

sources.author: Lysaght, Patricia
sources.work: The Banshee: The Irish Death Messenger
sources.description: Discusses Irish folklore surrounding death and spirits, providing context for the Jack-o'-lantern's association with lost souls.
sources.text: Discusses Irish folklore surrounding death and spirits, providing context for the Jack-o'-lantern's association with lost souls.

sources.author: Santino, Jack
sources.work: Halloween and Other Festivals of Death and Life
sources.description: Examines the cultural significance of Halloween and the evolution of its traditions, including the Jack-o'-lantern.
sources.text: Examines the cultural significance of Halloween and the evolution of its traditions, including the Jack-o'-lantern.

sources.author: Rogers, Nicholas
sources.work: Halloween: From Pagan Ritual to Party Night
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive history of Halloween, tracing the origins of the Jack-o'-lantern back to Samhain and Irish folklore.
